track_listing.details: B Side of 7” Single 'Working For The Yankee Dollar'

transcript.text: On 275 and 285. Also in stereo on VHF. This is National Radio 1.
Oh, hello there. Thomas the Vance here. TV on the radio.
Welcome once again to the Friday Rock Show.
Tonight, three ace sessions for you. Two from the archives.
The first by the Jeff Beck Group, recorded between 68 and 71.
And the second is from Queen. That was cut in 77.
Also, we have a repeat of the recent session by Def Leppard.
And this evening, the world radio premiere of tracks from the epic new double album by the Pink Floyd.
Also, we have new releases from Tom Petty and the Heartbreakers,
the Skids, Saxon, Rick Derringer and the Pop,
and classic tracks from Aerosmith, Hawkwind and also the Friday Night Connection.
And this evening, we start with a gentleman by the name of Sammy Hagar.
And that was Def Leppard on a track called Rock Brigade.
Especially to keep a particular gentleman happy by the name of Richard Cohen,
who lives at 98 Cecil Park in London.
And prior to that, the new single has just been released on Capitol by Sammy Hagar,
also to be found on the album Street Machine.
The single is called This Planet's On Fire.
Nine minutes after ten on the Friday Rock Show,
last week we did our sort of all-time, if you like, top ten,
based on the tracks that you sent in.
In other words, all the music was based on what you wanted to hear.
Some people have written in and said, would we repeat the chart?
So we will do that quite soon.
So if you want to jot it down, paper and pen to the floor.
And you'll hear it directly after this,
which is our first archive session, recorded on the 7th of the 3rd, 1967.
It's Jeff Beck.
The Jeff Beck Group, as they were way back in 1967,
comprising Ron Wood, Ray Cook, Rodney Stewart, would you believe,
and also, of course, that legendary man of the axe, Mr Jeff Beck,
and the Traxie Herbert tally man, Rock My Plimsoll,
I Know I'm Losing You, and an off-the-wall version of Hi-Ho Silver Lining.
Many people have written in requesting the Jeff Beck sessions, more later.
Let me just mention a few names.
It is John Walker of Reading, Richard Mackay of Oxford,
and Andrew Reading of Rotherham in South Yorkshire.
32 minutes after ten.
Now let's run down the chart as we played last week on the programme,
the top ten LP cuts as put together by you.
I'll go from ten to one.
Number ten was Shine On You Crazy Diamond by The Floyd.
Number nine, Starship Trooper by Yes.
Supper's Ready was number eight, that was by Genesis.
Stargazer was number seven, that was by Rainbow.
Did you spot the deliberate mistake?
No, not the fact that they allow me to present this programme,
but the fact that I said that that track came from the album Rainbow Rising.
I was wrong. I apologise.
It comes, of course, from the first Ritchie Blackmore album,
which was just called Ritchie Blackmore's Rainbow.
OK, on to number six, Smoke on the Water by Deep Purple.
At five, Xanadu by Rush.
At four, Layla by Derek and the Dominoes.
At three, Freebird by Lynyrd Skynyrd.
Child in Time was at two by Deep Purple.
And number one, it had to be Stairway to Heaven by Led Zeppelin.
And now for the first time on radio anywhere in the world, from the album The Wall, two tracks by The Floyd.
The Pink Floyd and two tracks from their mammoth double album, which is called The Wall.
The two tracks you heard were Hey You and the other track, In The Flesh, is the track that actually opens the double album.
I've heard the album umpteen times.
The last time I heard it was actually in the company of Roger Waters in a hotel bedroom, would you believe, in Beverly Hills, California on Monday.
And we had a long, long conversation in front of a tape recorder and he discussed with us exactly what the whole thing's all about.
Now, it really is a complicated, very complicated double album.
It takes more than one listen to get into,
but once you do get into it, I don't think you'll ever get out of it.
If you'd like to hear Roger Waters leading you behind the wall,
you can next week here on the Friday Rock Show,
when the whole programme is going to be devoted to the album
and also to Roger Waters leading us behind the wall.
And just as a little teaser, on Rock On tomorrow at 4 o'clock,
Mr Waters will be saying something,
and you can hear more from behind the wall.
I've got a stack of letters in front of me.
This one's from Mick Joyce, who's 15.
He lives at 11 Gerrard Avenue, Canley, in Coventry.
He's requested an archive session.
So has Nicky, or Fred Goff,
and everybody at the comprehensive school in Scunthorpe.
So, indeed, has Dave Telford of Bittern in Southampton,
John McLean of the Isle of Skye up there in Scotland,
and Paul Smith of Market Deeping in Peterborough.
And that archive session is queen.
absolutely amazing session that is to my ears and it must have been in the studio when it was
recorded on the 28th of the 10th of 1977 that was it's late and before that you heard spread
your wings just two out of four that we're playing tonight from that marvelous archive session
incidentally was produced by jeff griffin and balanced by robo robinson with not a synthesizer
in sight just about a quarter to 11 it is on the friday rock show i've got a little letter here
from John Stevenson of Greenacres, Glen Road, Tallwood,
Larbet in Stirlingshire,
who wants me at this precise moment of time
to tell a lady by the name of Mrs Joanna...
Mrs! I beg your pardon, Miss Joanna Morrison,
that he loves her.
They should be in a car returning from Badminton.
I've said it, Joanna, I nearly got you married to him, then.
Also, I must wish a happy birthday to a gentleman by the name of Nigel Paxton.
He's 17 today, and Nigel, Alison Slaven, loves you.
And here's Tom Petty and the Heartbreakers.
And that's The Skids,
with a track from a new double single that's just been released.
The, if you like, the A-side of the single is Working for the Yankee Dollar.
The track you heard there was Hymns from a Haunted Ballroom.
And it was produced by John Sparrow
and originally saw the light of day on the John Peel programme.
And it's nice to see that people are taking note of what the BBC do
in terms of recording after all these years
and putting them on records,
because they really deserve to be there, let's face it.
Nice one. That was by The Skids.
Prior to that, Tom Petty and the Heartbreakers
from their LP that's just out on MCA.
The LP's called Damn the Torpedoes,
and the track was entitled You Tell Me.
And I've got to say a very special hello to Dave Del Monte,
who's on a bit of a holiday at the moment,
with best wishes from Keith of Litherland in Merseyside.
And also I'd like to say hello to a bunch of gentlemen
who actually live 150 miles north of Scotland,
and they're all in Billet 15A.
That's Colin Holgate and his mates at RAF Saxe-Avord,
which is in Unst in the Shetlands,
which is a long way from here.
I hope it's warm up there. Here's Def Leppard.
Tricky in more ways than one, because we didn't realise it,
but there are two answers to that Friday night connection.
The tracks that we played were I Can't Turn You Loose by the Blues Brothers,
Hey Hey My My, Into the Black by Neil Young,
and Pigs on the Wing by Pink Floyd.
So the answer could have been Blues, Blues Brothers, right?
Hey Hey My My, Into the Black, and Pigs on the Wing by Pink Floyd.
In actual fact, that wasn't the answer at all,
but 50% of the people that actually wrote in thought it was.
We didn't even know that that answer was there.
We're better than we are.
The real answer was this.
I Can't Turn You Loose, Hey Hey My My and Pigs on the Wing
are songs that actually open the albums and close the albums.
And I think only two people got that absolutely right.
And the £10 record token goes to Dave Brown.
Congratulations, Dave.
At 11 Palmer Avenue, Cheadle in Cheshire.
You will be getting your well-earned £10 record token
just as soon as we can raise the money.
But now the licence has gone up, we can.
Which is good news.
And here now is Mr Brown's Friday night connection.
Just don't seem to have as much to lose
Strange how the night moves
Autumn closing in
Together by Dave Brown of Cheadle in Cheshire.
Somewhere in those three bits of music
could be in the lyrics, could be in the name of the artist,
could indeed be in the name of the songs
or melodies or whatever you like.
There is a connection.
If you think you know what it is,
then jot it down on a postcard or in a letter
and send it to this address.
Tommy Vance, The Friday Night Connection,
BBC Radio 1, London, W1A4, WW.
I said that quite slowly because I got a letter
from a gentleman by the name of Bobby Barclay in Dumfrieshire
who says he couldn't get the address.
So once again, it's The Friday Night Connection,
BBC Radio 1
London
W1A4WW
and don't forget
to put together
your own
Friday night
connection as well
and you might be
the recipient
big word
of the £10 record token
ok back to 76
and Jeff Beck
that was Jeff Beck
recorded 14th
of December
when his band
was made up of
Cozy Powell
on the drums of course
Bobby Tench on the vocals
Max Middleton on the piano
and Clive Chayman
I think it is
on the bass
and Mr Beck himself
on the guitar
beautiful
If you can ever get hold of his album, which is called Blow by Blow,
do so, because it's an absolute masterpiece.
And I played that archive session, especially for Neil Schofield
of Ascam in Furness in Cumbria,
and also John Burgess of Cartmill College, the University of Lancaster.
Oh, a quick reminder, too, that next week,
the whole programme is going to be devoted to the Pink Floyd
and their double album, two tracks which we played earlier on,
which are really sensational.
And the whole thing is going to be narrated, if you like, by Roger Waters.
he will lead us all behind the wall.
But right now, I'll lead you to Def Leppard.
A song written by Rick Nielsen of Cheap Trick,
but performed by Rick Derringer.
And it's a track from his new album,
which is entitled Guitars and Women.
It's on Blue Sky, and the track was It Must Be Love.
And before that, something by Def Leppard,
and that track was called Satellite.
And now, what you've all been waiting for,
or I would think most of you,
more by Queen,
including their amazing radio version
of We Will Rock You.
After this...
With best wishes from us to you,
That was Queen, circa 1977.
This Sunday's Star Special features one of Britain's most energetic voices.
He has just finished a mammoth tour of this country
and has stopped off before his European tour
to give us a taste of his own choice of music.
Meet the man behind this powerful voice
in Ian Gillan's Star Special on Sunday at 7 o'clock
here on Radio 1.
But right now, here's Aerosmith.
The 1977 album Draw the Line,
that was Aerosmith and Kings and Queens,
And that was most especially for Mad Martin and also for Majeed
and for Gordon and Mike and John Gesler and a gentleman by the name of Tom.
And they all attend a school called Bootham School, which is up there in York.
In front of me, I have a load, a load of letters and cards that ask me for this track.
So rather than read out the names of all the people,
you know who you are and you know who these people are too.
...the studio version of their single, which is kicking around at the moment outside the 40.
I'll rock on tomorrow. Judas Priest and Hugh Cornwall and loads of good music.
That's at four o'clock tomorrow.
Many thanks to our two engineers tonight, the two Pauls, and also thanks indeed to Sue, our secretary.
Throughout the week, the flag is kept hoisted by John Peel and Mike Reed in the evening.
Hope you enjoyed our sessions tonight. We thought they were great.
And now Teddy Wilson will speak to you.
Rock and roll!
Indeed.
I'd like to dedicate the whole of that program to a gentleman.
John Glasgow, who we laid to rest yesterday.
bass player and lovable
rogue and great friend
I'll see you tomorrow at 4 o'clock
It's midnight and we're together on all wavelengths
and VHF
and this is Vivian Stewart in the Midnight Newsroom
